text.title: Discovery Risk Framework
text.summary: Four risk categories for product ideas — value, usability, feasibility, business viability — each with technique options ranked by effort and confidence level.

rule.statement: Test risks in this order of priority: value risk first, then usability, then feasibility, then business viability. Kill ideas early on value risk before spending on later-stage tests.
rule.rationale: Value risk is the most common and cheapest to test. Building a technically feasible, usable product that nobody wants is the most expensive failure mode in product development.

rule.statement: Allocate 20–30% of team capacity to discovery. Never skip to delivery without addressing the highest-risk assumption.
rule.rationale: The cost of a failed feature is the engineering time plus the opportunity cost of what was not built instead. 20% discovery investment that prevents one failed 6-week feature pays for itself many times over.

list.item: Customer interviews — low effort, medium confidence — use for early-stage ideas
Fake door test (link to feature that does not exist yet) — low effort, medium confidence — use for testing demand
Landing page with signup — low effort, medium confidence — use for market interest
Concierge MVP (deliver value manually) — medium effort, high confidence — use for service-based products
Wizard of Oz (fake automation) — medium effort, high confidence — use for complex features
Paper prototypes — low effort, low confidence — use for early concept validation
Clickable prototypes — medium effort, medium confidence — use for flow validation
Usability testing with 5 users — medium effort, high confidence — use for design validation
A/B testing — high effort, very high confidence — use for optimization, not validation
Technical spike (1–2 days) — low effort, high confidence — use for unknown tech
Architecture review — low effort, medium confidence — use for system impact assessment
Proof of concept — medium effort, high confidence — use for critical path features
Financial modeling — low effort, medium confidence — use for revenue impact
Competitive analysis — low effort, medium confidence — use for market fit
Pilot program — high effort, very high confidence — use only for major investments
